story:
Dolly Jameson is a woman of routine: 52, single, faithful cat named ZoZo, gospel hums, tea at dawn, bus at the same time every morning. Her alarm always rings at 6:15 a.m. She stretches, prays, shuffles to the kitchen while ZoZo—fat, grey, and supervisory—flicks his tail in time.

But one Tuesday, she wakes to rain, not her alarm. It’s 7:02. The plug has slipped loose from the wall clock. ZoZo descends the stairs deliberately, as if time belongs to him. Dolly races out, misses the bus, and steps straight into a day that refuses to be kind. Five speeding cars splash her in perfect, punishing rhythm. An old man under an umbrella considers her dripping state and says, “You look baptized.” At work, squelching and smeared, she learns she’s first to present to the shareholders. In the restroom, her reflection blinks out of sync, then smiles without her.

She squeezes her eyes shut—and wakes in bed to a cheerful 6:15 alarm. ZoZo licks a paw; the rain has stopped. Dream? Maybe. Until ZoZo speaks. “Don’t miss your bus, Dolly.” She drops her tea. He asks if she wants to test reality. She pinches herself. It hurts. She runs out anyway. The rain returns on cue. Five cars, five splashes. The same old man says, “You again?” At work, the mirror speaks: “I’m you. Or you’re me.” “I just want to wake up.” “Then close your eyes.”

She does, and wakes again—this time with the alarm flickering: 6:15, 7:02, 8:45, blank. ZoZo pads down the stairs, each step echoing. The stove light glows; her lunch simmers and smells burnt. “I left that yesterday,” she whispers. Her phone insists it’s Wednesday. “Time is flexible when you don’t believe in it,” ZoZo says. Mr. Banda calls: “The shareholders are waiting.” Dolly blinks and she’s already in front of the projector, slides up, throat full of cotton. Everyone turns toward the window. Rain sheets down. A bus splashes across the glass. ZoZo’s voice floats in: “You missed the bus again.”

She screams—and wakes in a hospital. Tubes, machines, a kind nurse. “You’ve been asleep for two days, Ms. Jameson. You fainted at work.” Relief—until she reads the nurse’s name tag: ZOZO. Rain starts outside.

Two weeks later, Dolly rebuilds her routine. Tea. Light chores. She repeats: It was only a dream. Sometimes ZoZo watches her too intently. One night she hears him whisper, “Again.” She tells herself she imagined it.

Morning. The alarm blares at exactly 6:15. Normal. Except ZoZo isn’t on the bed. From the stairs comes that slow, deliberate descent. The clock stutters to 7:02. Rain begins inside the bedroom. A horn blares. SPLASH. Water hits her face from a leaking ceiling. “You’re late, Dolly,” ZoZo says. She laughs, a little wild now, and walks downstairs into a house made of mirrors. Infinite Dollys fill the walls—one crying, one brushing her hair, one nailing the presentation. “Which one is real?” “All of them. None. You decide,” ZoZo answers. “I want to wake up.” “Then sleep.” “And if I’m asleep?” “Then dream better.”

Later—if there is a later—her neighbor knocks. No answer. The landlord opens a spotless home: teacup on the table, lunch packed on the stove, alarm blinking 6:15. ZoZo sits on the stairs, eyes bright, tail swishing. “She finally caught the bus,” he says.
